Hello Cluster Family,
I just wanted to share my current experience with you all. First, a little back story, I have been a sufferer of clusters since I was 15 years old, I have seen so many different doctors and been given more pills than I could ever count. None of it helped. I had a cluster 1 week after giving birth to my youngest son, and all I remember was sitting on the bathroom floor, sleep deprived, in agony, legitimately wondering if I was going to lose my grip on reality and go insane.
Earlier this year I finally saw a neurologist that officially diagnosed me with CH, and I remember I immediately felt relief, because I had thought for years that this is what I had, but no doctor would diagnose me because the disorder is "primarily in men". It only took seconds for the relief to suddenly be taken over by despair. An incurable neurological disorder, that there is no known cause or cure. I spent several months in severe depression and anxiety, accompanied by insomnia from fear of sleep, as that is when my attack occur.
My clusters are normally triggered by extreme stress, or seasonal changes, so when summer started giving way to fall, my normal 6 week cluster period started. For 3 weeks I was going through 2 oxygen tanks a week, being woken up hourly from midnight to 7 am in excruciating pain. After being shamed into taking more prescription meds by my neurologist, I took verapamil for the first time. I took it at 5pm, and by 730 I was in the worst pain I have ever been in. I know this cluster was brought on by the medication because I do not normally get attacks in the evening. My clusters are very predictable, and always occur at night once I have gone to sleep. This attack very nearly put me in the hospital, and the only reason it didn't was because my husband gave me something that calmed me down enough to be able to withstand the pain.
The following day, I posted my first post on this page, asking for any "alternative" remedies that others had found helpful. I was put in contact with Batch, who gave me information on the Vitamin D3 regimen. (Which I have linked below)
https://vitamindwiki.com/Cluster+headaches+substantially+reduced+by+10,000+IU+of+Vitamin+D+in+80+percent+of+people
Unfortunately, I did not have all the items in the regimen in my medicine cabinet, but I live in the Pacific North West, and as we do get less sun here than in other parts of the county, so I had previously purchased a  vitamin D3 dropper with 5,000ui per serving, which I had not taken because I am habitually bad at remembering to take medication or vitamins. So I decided to start taking two doses in the morning. I figured it couldn't hurt.
Now remember, at this time I am at about the halfway point in my normal 6 week cluster period. I started taking the vitamin D3, and did not feel any different the first night. However, the second night I was only woken up by about 3 clusters, all of which were about 50% less painful than normal. The following night, I was

First post but have been reading the board for years. I wanted to share what has been working for me for the past year and a half. Usual caveats- not a doctor, your mileage may vary, and this is just my experience.
For some background, I have had cluster headaches for going on 12 years. Every year has a minimum of two episodes that last 8-10 weeks. Each episode will have typically 2-3 headaches per day. Emgality and verapamil didn’t prevent or stop clusters for me. I have had strong side effects from medicines like sumatriptan and Topamax and refused to use them any longer.
I tried the clusterbuster program and had mild success on my on days, but immediately fell back into headaches on the days off with no lasting relief.
Last year I tried the below supplement routine and have used it for my last three cycles. I have had shadows on the program but have been headache free for 18 months so far. It’s based on research showing low allopregnanolone levels in cluster headache patients and theorizing that the low levels are a part of the headache process
Treatment Plan:
I start at first sign of headaches or shadows.
Reducing the Severity of Headaches and Shadows:
aka preventing the trigeminal nerve from getting angry
Cetirizine:
10mg twice daily (h1 receptor antagonist. Low potential to cross blood brain barrier however some research shows it will slightly. Chosen over benedryl due to lack of sedation and cognitive impairment)
Famotidine:
20mg twice daily (h2 receptor antagonist, crosses blood brain barrier)
Quercetin:
500mg twice daily. (reduced global histamine response)
Raising Allopregnanolone levels and improving HTPA function:
to help fill in the gaps where the body is currently failing in production
Vitamin D3 with K2:
10,000mg of Vitamin D3 with 300mg of K2, taken once daily.
Pregnenolone:
50mg once daily to convert to allopregnanolone. Research shows pregnenolone readily converts to allopregnanolone. This can be titrated up as needed to reduce headache frequency and severity. I have gone up to 300mg during a cycle, and there is some research showing that it is well tolerated up to 500mg per day.
DHEA:
50mg daily to encourage the conversion of pregnenolone to allopregnanolone so it doesn’t convert down the testosterone pathway.
Ashwagandha:
250mg sensoril for reduced cortisol
Abortive:
At the first sign of a headache
Caffeine:
from coffee or tea. I avoid caffeine sources such as energy drinks or supplements containing niacin or other vasodilators, which is counter productive since we want the vasoconstriction of caffeine
Every 5 days step down pregnenolone dosage. If shadows return, step back up and repeat.
With this I have been able to keep my headaches away and combat shadows with a cup of coffee.
This is my third cycle using this program and I waited to post it until I had repeat success. I understand that this could be unique to me, however the repeated success I’ve had has made me want to share this protocol in case it can help anyone else.

Hi everyone.
I last posted about a year and a half ago sharing my experiences with DMT as an abortive, thread can be found below. I wanted to log back in and share some experiences of my recent cluster attacks where again DMT has proven to work well, just in case my experiences prove useful for others
https://clusterbusters.org/forums/topic/7444-dmt-first-time-finding-an-effective-abortive/#comment-71740
Ive been waiting 1.5 years to have another cluster to see if DMT would again be effective and a few days ago I had my chance. I woke up at 5am with a small cluster (tried oxygen, seemed to work) but then an hour later at 6am I had a proper attack. I pulled out
the DMT vape
Ive kept in my bedside table, loaded it with wax and had a small amount (as Im not experienced with DMT I was worried about having too much and tripping while in pain). Didnt really feel anything and the cluster persisted. About a minute later I had a larger inhale and this time held. A few seconds after exhaling my fingers and toes started to tingle and the headache pain halved in about 10 seconds. After a minute without any serious tripping I had a 3rd inhale of about the same. The ceiling swam a bit and I saw some fractals and just like that the cluster was gone. Literally in a matter of seconds.
30 minutes later I was driving to work with no discernible lingering of the headache or the DMT. About 2 hours later I experienced a shadow while at work but it didnt evolve into a full blown headache.
The next day I was driving when the headache started around 6:30am so I pulled over, hit the DMT and waited it out. I was a bit too scared of having too much in a semi-public place so I never felt the fingers and toes tingle and it just knocked the headache down a few notches, but was able to get through the day without any of my colleagues being any the wiser to what was happening to me.
I also take verapamil twice a day when my clusters start as that seems to help shorten the period and Ive now been a few days without another attack. I'll stay on the verapamil for a few weeks and Im hoping I'll make it through this period without any further attacks. Im careful to not drink and to try and get as much rest as possible when Im in an attack period as well.
It has made such a difference to my mental state to have an abortive option. I run a small business and work as a contractor, so the fact I was able to still make it to work on time last week, without my client having a clue that I was unwell and would potentially be unreliable as a result, is such a huge win. I'm organising to meet with my cluster-specialising Neurologist to walk him through DMT as my experiences now show some clear data of it working as an abortive.
Feel free to fire off any questions below and Ill answer as best I can. I dont have any idea about actually acquiring DMT unfortunately, it was a gift from a friend who has since passed away so Im just being careful not to use much of what I have.
Cheers

Hey Clusterbusters
My name is Alec and I'm 33 years old. I had my first cluster headache at 12 and was diagnosed at 16 by a neurologist where I live in Australia. I have been a member of several forums a few years back but haven't really been active in support groups for the last 5 years or so and I need to vent. I saw the "Beast" documentary on YouTube a few days ago and Cluster Busters seems like an awesome group - I'd love to become an active member here and get involved as much as I can. I have just had a KIP 10 cluster attack with a complete absence of pain (see my story below), I could just feel the immense pressure and I can tell what pain level it is even though it's masked. I haven't had a KIP10 in around 6 months or so and it literally just went away, so I'm still pumping full of adrenalin and just want to talk a bit about my last few months.
When I was diagnosed at 16, I attempted the full array of then first and second line treatment options. All the standard medications such as verapamil, triptans etc. My headaches never responded to any preventatives or abortives and as a last resort I was placed on opioids. At first I was taking codeine and then graduated to oxycodone. I want to make it clear from the outset that I do not recommend opioids for cluster headaches sufferers, but every situation is unique and for me opioids became my primary way of coping with my headaches. Of course, the dose continually increased and to be completely honest up until around a month ago they never really provided me with much relief from the physical pain at all. They did help me markedly with the emotional distress and turmoil associated with treatment-refractory cluster headaches which unfortunately for me became chronic at around 25 years old.
I understand that everybody's perception of pain is different and that some of us have episodic clusters and others (like me) have chronic. I have to say though that after doing research on cluster headaches online, either there are many people out there who claim to have the condition who clearly do not OR I happen to suffer a lot more than others. For me, cluster headaches are the worst imaginable pain and trying to describe them is pointless. I do not understand how somebody could have cluster headaches and mistakenly think they have migraines for years before a correct diagnosis - for me, the pain of a cluster headache is so distinct and unique.
The toll these headaches have had on my life is catastrophic. I have been in 2x long-term relationships with people I loved and both ended due to my inability to be a normal person due to my pain. I have tried so hard to maintain employment and have worked in several industries including law enforcement, finance and legal services. All of my attempts at employment have ended due to my pain and I have been on long-term disability now for several years. Hello my fellow Clusterhead brothers and sisters,
I hope your day isn't too painful. Fist bumps, back pats and hugs all round to everyone fighting the beast.
Episodic CH sufferer for 15 yrs, I'm 46, UK.
Usual cycle is Oct/Nov to March/April, reaching a frenzy of agony in the middle over Christmas. So 6 months per year, 1-4 per day ranging from 1-3 hrs. So 12 hrs per day at peak when meds aren't working.
My latest cycle started this July, after a rare year long remission. The beast returned ravenous, and got to back to work devouring my eyeball several times a day....
I'm a long time user of MM for CH with mixed results. I think I may have completely busted a couple of cycles (who knows, the beast is so random), other times it's had no effect (and not through the lack of trying, trust me. I have no problem munching away on a megadose or three) Other times I get temporary, but significant relief for a few days only.
My staple has been zomyg, with a MM dose when I can get a window to detox. This kept it manageable, but after 6 months of zomyg spray use, my attacks went into overdrive.
Several attacks a day, reaching new levels of pain I never imagined possible. My entire body would buzz while I bounced off the walls moaning in agony, and every tool of torture imaginable went to work on the nerves behind my eye with sadistic precision.
I ended up in an ambulance to the ER. Scanned again for tumours/burst things in brain.  'Everything is fine'. Aside from having zero quality of life and thinking if I get run over by that bus, or fall off this balcony, that wouldn't be so bad, would it....?
I've always steered away from Verapamil. Heart pills? Big doses? ECGs? Scary. The 320mg starting dose they recommend is 50x what my 86 yr old dad takes!
But this time, with all my other weapons ineffective, and nothing to lose, the doc pusuaded me to try it.
My god, I wish I'd done so years ago! By the time I was on 160mg, so just a week in and taking 1/2 the recommended baseline dose - I was getting shadows, annoying pressure and runny eye/nose only. Very little pain. At 200mg, nada, nothing.  It's like the relief of summer has come early.
That horrible sense of foreboding and defeat I feel every minute of the day, gone. And it's definitely the pills, I've tested stopping taking them and the pain creeps back until I start again. But not a single attack since getting to 160mg over a month ago.
As for sides effects, I feel a little less lively first thing in the morning, but pain free bliss puts the spring back in my step very quickly.
Sorry to anyone who's read this, tried Verapamil and it didn't work for you, or you can't take it. I read it only helps 40% of people to varying degrees. I guess that's why most people are here. I was voluntarily avoiding it, kind of managing on MM and now I  regret that. Verapamil has been miraculous and without needing silly dosage levels.

m still a fan of MM, and will try and keep it to natural and bust next season, but it feels

21 year Episodic. Clusters used to happen every 9 months, lasted 1 month with 1 or two attack / day lasting 1 hour each. The cycle would be like clockwork, almost to the day. Back then I was treating with maxalt wafers only, and running cold water on my head. Eventually was prescribed amitriptyline, which unsurprisingly didn't do anything. Sometimes my cycle would
end
with a day-long attack. Different from a regular cluster headache, but, still on the cluster side. Really weird. Anyway, I would often end up in the ER with these ending attacks, but, they were always bad news / good news. Bad because I knew they would last at least 24 hours; and were brutal (not as bad as a CH attack, but, pretty unbearable due to the length of the attack and no sleep). Good because I knew the cycle would end after that one weird one. Eventually saw my first neurologist in 2012 after a trip to the ER (10 years after my CHs started). I've had all of the required brain scans, etc. and got the official diagnosis. Neurologist recommended maxalt wafers, home oxygen, prednisone taper at the start of a cycle, and verapamil for the duration of the cycle and for 6 weeks after it clears. Family doctor wouldn't prescribe me any of those things except for Maxalt. Said everything else was too risky.
Found a new Family Doctor 2 years later (2014). Got me hooked up with another Neurologist, and got the same treatment plan recommended. Got all of my scripts filled, etc.
Over the years, my cycles spread out longer (longer remission), but, more attacks while in cycle. One cycle, I didn't realize the risks of too many triptans (was taking 2, 3, sometimes 4 per day that cycle). Ended up getting really bad rebound headaches. Cycle lasted much longer, and I ended up getting upwards of 8 attacks per day. After that cycle, and realizing what had happened; I've tried to avoid the triptans whenever possible. I rely on home o2, and only use a maxalt nasal spray if I'm out and don't have access to o2 or any other options.
Started the D3 regimen in 2019, and have been taking all of the cofactors and everything daily since then.
For the last few cycles, my treatment plan has been:
1) 13 day prednisone taper at the onset of a cycle; 60mg 2 days, 50mg 3 days, 40mg 3 days, 30mg 2 days, 20mg 2 days, 10mg 1 day
2) Verapamil - 120mg x 3 / day
3) Home o2 - 25lpm through Cluster o2 mask (coupled with the odd sip of really cold ice water through a straw)
4) Rizatriptan Nasal spray only for emergencies (5mg)
5) Still doing the D3 regimen
6) Have tried adding melatonin in before bed (10mg)
7) Energy drinks to try to help clear shadows
8) Ice-pack on the head for shadows, and sometimes during attacks (other times I can't stand it)
9) No alcohol
The prednisone taper has killed 2 cycles right at the start. One cycle it didn't completely kill it (the cycle above with the triptan overuse); and I ended up doing a 2nd prednisone taper, which did end it

Now without going into every single detail of what happened to me in the last ten years, I wanted to give a brief update on my journey, as I realize that the knowledge built on this site is for a big part generated by anecdotal information, here's my humble contribution:

Hi there!
I am new to the board and tbh kind of new to clusters as well.
So let me introduce myself.
My name is Pieter-Jan, Belgian, 33YO, former mechanic, whisky-lover
and this nickname used to be quite a well-known name in BMW-forums of Europe.
Never thought I'd use the name in this context, but here I am.
I'm probably quite lucky compared to a lot of you guys.
It all started in Octobre 2014, with a 'severe' headache. I never had any headaches,
and yes, I am one of those guy who thought migraine is a lame excuse to stay home from work. Boy, was I wrong.
But then, I had a few of those attacks and they were bad enough to make me go to a doctor.
I had an MRI with nothing to be found. the attacks stopped after a few and i stopped worrying about it.
Some time later a belgian CH-patient is shown in the media and i got to know the term 'clusterheadache',
which me me wonder if that could have been what i experienced.
I shrugged it off and told myself not to be a baby, it was impossible my attacks were the same as such a serious condition.
The more time passed, the more I knew i was being a crybaby back in the day.
Until March 24 of this year, an easy sundayeve with the family.
At 8pm it struck. Excruciating pain, stabbing in the back of my eye, me trying to push my eye in my head,
till I was crying, rolling around in the coach.
At 9.30pm it stopped, completely gone. my eye looked like a blood vessel had popped, my skin red and hot,
but that was all the evidence left.
That moment I knew it was the same thing like 2014, googled it and found a sh*load of information about it.
The descriptions were a perfect fit.
exactly one week later i had the proof, cause there was the second attack.
A few days later they started daily and before the next weekend multiple times/day and at night.
I'll spare you guys the doctor stories, you all know how those go.
I was lucky to be taken serious and got to a neurologist within 3 days.
He listened to my story and took a seat.
If you know, you know In Europe doctors are trained to bring 'bad news' such as patients passing, cancer, serious deseases... etc.
Step 1 of this 'bad news conversation' is to sit down next to the patient.
After some explanation he got right to it and gave me a GON-injection.
which started working after 2 days.
I didn't have (severe) pain from the attacks. I still feel they are happening and feel an occasional stab in the eye,
but I can handle the pain.
One thing that's harder to handle is the shadow. Since the injection I have an almost constant headache at the level of a brainfreeze.
Verapamil helped a little bit but made my legs swell very fast to the point my knee hurts enough to make me limp.
Caffeine-gum and energydrinks don't do anything, except from keeping me awake at night.
Since I read a lot of stories I decided maybe i don't have to suffer for years like a lot of you...
Maybe i don't have to try 50 different medicines..
I already ordered some truffles. Maybe it's not enough since i ordered

TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS: CLUSTER HEADACHE AND RELATED CONDITIONS
Peter J. Goadsby
*
, in
Neurology and Clinical Neuroscience
, 2007
Verapamil
Verapamil
was first reported to be an effective preventive agent for cluster headache by Meyer and Hardenberg.
84
In an open-label trial with
verapamil
at doses of 160 to 720 mg/day in five CCH patients, a reduction in the mean monthly headache frequency was reported in all patients. In another open-label trial with
verapamil
at doses of 240 to 600 mg/day for ECH and 120 to 1200 mg/day for CCH, an improvement of more than 75% was noted in 33 (69%) of 48 patients.
85
A double-blind, crossover trial in which
verapamil
, 360 mg/day, was compared with the then standard prophylactic drug, lithium, 900 mg/day, each given for 8 weeks, demonstrated equivalent effects in the 24 CCH patients who completed the trial.
86
Verapamil
and lithium were superior to placebo.
Verapamil
caused fewer side effects and had a shorter latency period. In a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, the efficacy of
verapamil
, 360 mg/day over a 2-week period, was evaluated in 26 patients with ECH.
87
A statistically significant reduction in headache frequency and analgesic consumption occurred in the
verapamil
-treated patients, with a greater reduction in the second week of treatment.
Verapamil
is the preventive drug of choice for both CCH and ECH, the latter when the bout is sufficiently long to establish a suitable dose. Clinical experience has clearly demonstrated that higher doses are needed for cluster headache than for cardiological indications. Dosages commonly employed range from 240 to 960 mg/day in divided doses.
Verapamil
can cause heart block by slowing conduction in the atrioventricular node,
88
as demonstrated by prolongation of the A-H interval.
89
In observing for P-R interval prolongation on the electrocardiogram, clinicians can monitor potential development of heart block, although it is a coarse measure. No formal guidelines are available. After obtaining a baseline electrocardiogram, the author and colleagues start patients on 80 mg three times daily, and the total daily dose is increased in increments of 80 mg every 10 to 14 days. An electrocardiogram is obtained before each increment and at least 10 days after the last dose change. The dosage is increased until the cluster attacks are suppressed, side effects intervene, or the maximum dose of 960 mg/day is achieved. It is unproved clinical experience that standard preparations of
verapamil
are more effective than the modified-release formulations.
58,
90
Constipation is the most common side effect, but dizziness, ankle swelling, nausea, fatigue, hypotension, and bradycardia may also occur. Gingival enlargement is a recognized problem that needs prompt attention from a dentist.
91
β Blockers should not be given concurrently.

Harmala Alkaloids are substances found in varying degrees in plants like Syrian Rue, Banisteriopsis caapi vine, Passionflower and Tobacco (I'm sure there are more)Â  The two with the highest known amounts are the Syrian Rue seeds and the Banisteriopsis Caapi vines.
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmala_alkaloid
The major alkaloids involved are Harmine, Harmaline, and Tetrahydroharmine.Â  These are all Beta Carbolines, They all inhibit the MAO A enzyme, and they all are considered RIMA's--Reversible Inhibitors of MAO subtype A.Â (For more info on RIMA's you can look into the pharmaceutical anti-depressant moclobomide, which has MAO A enzyme as it's mechanism of action.)Â  Â All 3 substances are completely legal in the U.S., as are both the Caapi Vine and Rue seeds.Â  I know I have read that they are hitting some of the same receptors as Psilocybin and LSA, but right now I can't find my sources.
But first we'll touch on MAO inhibitors in general.Â  Everything you hear about them sounds scary, everything seems to say, "Do Not Mix With MAO inhibitors"Â  We need to know that the substances contained in Caapi vines and Syrian Rue seeds are very different drugs than the old style pharmaceutical MAO inhibitors that were usually used for depression.Â  These older MAO inhibitors would inhibit TWO enzymes, MAO A and MAO B, where as the RIMA's only inhibit ONE enzyme, MAO A.Â  This is important because that means you still have an active MAO B enzyme, which makes your body perfectly able to still metabolize Tyramine.Â  With the older MAO inhibitors that inhibited BOTH enzymes you are not able to metabolize tyramine, this can lead to hypertensive crisis, and this is the biggest warning you will hear about with MAO inhibiting drugs.Â  Just remember--THIS IS NOT AN ISSUE WITH THE NATURAL MAO A INHIBITING DRUGS LIKE RUE OR CAAPI VINES.Â  The biggest thing that made me more comfortable with these drugs was when I realized that I have been taking them all my life without knowing it.Â  Certain foods, like chocolate, have this effect naturally andÂ  you'll never notice it.Â  Â Â In fact, Terence Mckenna claims that ALL tryptamines are RIMA's--this means the mushrooms, LSD, and LSA containing seeds that we are taking are all having this effect on us.Â  I can confirm this effect from the experience of smoking DMT while on mushrooms.Â  (More on that later)
So no risk of "hypertensive crisis"Â  Just forget about it.Â  There is the theory that if you are on RIMA's and eat lots and lots of tyramine containing foods that eventually you could "overwhelm" the system and cause this crisis, but I have NEVER heard of this actually happening.Â  From what I've read it seems more like people have been scared of MAO inhibitors for a long time and are reluctant to let go of that fear.Â  One of the biggest sources of tyramine is chees

Hey folks!
First let me say that I'm grateful to find these forums, and the resources in the relevant stickied topics/links already taught me plenty I didn't know about this horrible thing, and some hope to be had in a moment I really, really need.
I thought I'd share my history with CH, since even those who empathize and care for me don't really understand what it's like. I was one of those people that almost never got headaches naturally, and even when I was hungover it was not that common, unless I had a LOT to drink, which predictably became less and less common as I aged. I'm 29 years old and I live in Brazil, and in 2017 I got my first episode of CH, and of course I had no idea what that was, it lasted a week, and came at 3h30 in the morning and went away exactly an hour later, like the world's most brutalizing clockwork. After three days I was absolutely desperate, as anything I'd take had no effect whatsoever, before or during the attack, but I couldn't get an appointment at a neurologist covered by my insurance plan until a week later, so I endured the agony. Fortunately, he did know what CH was, which after a cursory reading of the experiences on this forum seems rather uncommon, but unfortunately he didn't know how to treat it properly. He prescribed Naratriptan each day before bed, and intranasal oxygen at a hospital, but I didn't have time to practice any of it, as it had gone away and I hoped it would never return.
7 months later, in February of 2018, came the second episode, exactly like the first one. The Naratriptan did exactly nothing, and as I lived alone, not in a million years would I be able to get to a hospital by myself, much less in time to be treated for something that lasted an hour. Fuck, I couldn't even get to the bathroom. But despite that, I had one hope: That it would only be six days more of agony, and then hopefully it would leave me alone, and it did. After that I went to another neurologist, and he recommended Sumatriptans, but otherwise mantained the same useless oxygen prescription. A year later in 2019 it came back again, same ol' story, and for the first two days I tried the Sumatriptan pills, which again did nothing, but as I was recently diagnosed with T2 Diabetes, I had a lot of syringes at home, so I bought Sumatriptan vials and started to apply it myself, starting with small doses and adjusted, until I came to the conclusion that 4mg was enough. I'd apply it and smoke enough weed to down an elephant, and I'd get a fast relief and fall asleep soon after, although I'd feel like shit the next day, but hey, it was just for a week and it was lightyears better than going through that agony over and over again, plus I thought that banging my head against the wall couldn't be good for my brain.
And then came this nightmare of a year we just left behind. As it started in April, I was prepared, already had a stash saved, and even though my Diabetes is thankfully on remission and I don't use insulin anymore, I sa

Dear Clusterheads,
The time has come to introduce myself to our beloved clusterheads family.
I was observing You for long time, but now I feel obliged to give my input.
My name is Radoslaw and I'm from Poland, suffering for CH since 2016 (maybe 2015, I don't remember properly). First two years without proper diagnose, but with small 1-2 weeks episodes during autumn each year. In 2018 I had 4 weeks terrible headaches, so I started my journey through neurologists. With help form the web knowledge I made a diagnose which was later confirmed by doctors - cluster headaches. Firstly doctors proposed me verapamil, but after cardiac research they refused to give it to me because of bradicardia ( a lot of wrestling and boxing in the teenage years made my hearth rate about 50-55). I didn't want to take lithium and gabapentin because of side effects ( no other therapy was known here in PL)
In 2019 in June my cluster appeared once again, stronger than before, so I desperately tried to find new solution. I found new doctor ( who isn't neurologist but is a specialist in headaches - brilliant guy !). Together we decided to take a risk and start with verap 480, under direct control of electro-cardio-diagrams. BOOM ! worked perfectly. My hearth rate felt down to 45-50 ( during night even to 40) but I felt good, without side effects, so I decided to continue it. For next 2 years I even forgot that I have this terrible disease ( for my bad...). Unfortunately I didn't stop taking verap after cluster period, but stayed on it for the whole year. In 2021 each attempt to reduce verap caused some shadows and single attacks, so my doctor suspected that I went to chronic version ( today I'm wondering that maybe I'm not chronic, but such long period of verap caused attacks when I tried to stop it - slowly as recommended).
In 2022 in June ( when I was on 480 verap) cluster hit with double power. I increased verap to 960, but it was very risky. It took 4 weeks to stop the cluster but I got also atrial block 1 stage at the end of cycle. I felt really dizzy, so 960 is little too much for me. Since that time, when I was on 480 some single attacks appeared during the year.
Today I'm 34 and I'm really concerned about using verapamil with bradicardia for such a long time. I have concerns wbout hearth pacemaker in the future, so for that reason I decided month ago to stop with verap and to go with regime D and busting with witamin L.
I know the oxygen method, I have 3 tanks at home with proper flowmeter and 1 small tank that I have always in my car. This is my first choise for attack. I'm using also a lot of caffeine in energy drinks.
To be honest I had some journeys with witamin L in my early 20s, so I wasn't worried about psychedelic trip ( maybe it was even good reason to try it once again).
On 8th of April I started regime D ( initial D level was 22, so very low).
I took first dose of L (50mg) on 11th of April ( last day of verap 120 and clear of triptans since beginning of Marc

First CH's started November of 2015.  Thought they were a result of a hangover, caffeine withdrawals, or dehydration.  True cycles started to emerge in spring 2016.  Misdiagnosed by ER as Migraine.  Correctly diagnosed as CH later.  Went through the cycle of preventative and abortives.  Benefits were often limited, side effects were intolerable.  Methodically tried elimination diets, med shifts, etc.  Most with some degree of correlation, but none with lasting relief.  Latest preventative was visiting a local chiropractor.  He noted in Chiropractic school, a member of his cohort was a CH suffer and they focused a bit of Chiropractic intervention with success. I gave it a try.  (Will add notes at the bottom on the impact of other meds)
When engaging chiropractor, I chose to not disclose much beyond CH.  He ran an x-ray panel and review with me.  In his review, he commented that the misalignment of C1 to C3, and other issues in the cervical vertebrae and other markers would lead him to believe I had suffered whiplash ~10 years earlier.  I had not disclosed in Dec of 2002, I had a head-on collision that I chose not to pursue medical treatment for.  He proceeded with a 3-month cycle of adjustments, 3x weekly, with a focus on addressing cervical issues.
In the 3 month cycle, which started about 2 weeks after the start of my CH cycle, the headaches stopped almost entirely.  3 months in, I have a few breaks through CHs,  average 1 or fewer per day (which is miraculous as on cycle I am consistently at 3x daily, on a schedule [2:31 am, 6:00 am, and 2:00 pm]).  I've noted that, when I miss the Chiro adjustments (due to work, etc) greater than 4 days, there is a higher potential to suffer more than 1 CH.
As we all know, CH triggers and resolution is mostly about correlation, as causality is not yet well established.  But thus far, chiropractic adjustments, coupled with stretches, shoulder and neck muscle exercises focusing on extension / relaxation, with heat on neck seem to have demonstrated the most effective treatment path for prevention without side effects.
Hope it helps. I am in Beaverton Oregon and see a chiro here.  I can provide a referral if interested.

Hi all - Quick update.

As stated above, I got a second course of DHE infusions on December 1-3.  I actually found a private infusion center covered by my insurance (called Paragon Infusions, which is located in a number of states) that will do the 3 day DHE course.  The doc prescribed toradol, magnesium, reglan and DHE.  That keeps me there for almost 3 hours.  I declined everything but the DHE and reglan and can get in and out in just shy of an hour, which, if you can do it early enough in the morning, can get you to work by 10.  Not bad.
I took the 300 mg of Emgality on December 5.  First, the shadows and other sensations that I was having over the course of the day in November effectively eliminated during December.  That notwithstanding, I was still having breakthrough hits, but only at night, and only every 3-6 days during December and the first week of January. Also, the intensity was way, way down, and sometimes the hits were painless (wake up, eyes closed, could feel my face sweating and mind racing, usually fall back asleep, light shadowing the next day).  I am now 5 weeks post second dose, and the cycle seems to be winding down (now that I’ve said that I’ll probably have an attack tonight…..).  I haven’t had a drink since October, but generally things up there feel relatively normalized.
The bad news is the Emgality has made me severely constipated.  It doesn’t touch Verapamil’s constipation, but it’s still pretty awful.  Emgality’s half life is really long, so I don’t really know how long the constipation is going to last.  It does seem to be letting up some, and I’m hopeful that the discomfort will be more or less disposed of by the end of the month.  We’ll see.
My neuro wants me to stay on Emgality or some CGRP blocker full time.  We’re trying to figure out what dose I can tolerate all the time without the side effects (I’ll put up with a month or two or even three of severe constipation and discomfort, but not ALWAYS).  The plan is to try the 120 mg migraine dose and see if I can live with that.  We hope - without any evidence - that perhaps blocking enough CGRP in me full time will stop the cascading reactions that start the cycles to begin with.
The reality check, here, is that I’ve tried this before and it failed miserably.  In 2020, I felt the cycle ramping up a little in July so did 300 mg of Emgality in July, August and September.  The cycle broke through anyway and, by the beginning of October, I was in a hell of a lot of pain.  That cycle took 4 prednisone tapers and 9 months of verapamil to break.  I have theories about why it was so bad (tons of prednisone, tons of work-related stress, all turning into tons of personal stress).  What I think really made the difference this time were (1) the DHE

I’m 56 years old, and I suffer from episodic cluster headaches. It took years to get properly diagnosed, and by the time I did, the condition had already taken over large parts of my life. Anyone who has experienced cluster headaches understands that this isn’t just a headache—it’s a cycle of severe, debilitating pain that dictates how you live day to day.
Before I found an effective system, I relied on multiple treatments just to function. I used Rizatriptan, which could take up to an hour to work. Then I moved to Sumatriptan nasal spray, which worked in about 25 minutes, and eventually injectable Sumatriptan, which was faster. I also used high-flow oxygen at home set to 15 liters per minute, and I carried a portable unit at work. Even with all of this, my life revolved around anticipating attacks—how many injections I had left, where I could go, and whether I could even leave the house.
Preventive therapy helped stabilize things. I currently take Verapamil daily and use Emgality monthly. These treatments made a difference, but they didn’t completely free me from the cycles.
After doing a lot of research, I came across information from groups like Clusterbusters and studies being done at Yale. I read about people using psilocybin in different ways—microdosing, moderate dosing, and high-dose “cycle-breaking” approaches. After careful consideration, I chose the high-dose method.
My first experience was a full weekend: 7 grams on Friday, 7 grams on Saturday, and 7 grams on Sunday. What I noticed immediately, the intensity was way less and they started to come back slowly, not immediate. Another surprising change was how my oxygen therapy worked. Before, it would take 15 to 20 minutes to stop an attack. After that weekend, when I have any symptoms at all, oxygen worked in about 5 minutes. That alone was life-changing.
Over the course of a year, I repeated this same three-day protocol two more times, spacing each weekend several months apart. Each time, the result was consistent: the cluster headaches stopped. When they eventually returned, they came back slower, less intense, and much more manageable.
At one point, I stopped my preventive medications to see what would happen. The cluster headaches came back. When I restarted my medications, they went away again. That confirmed for me that my baseline treatment—Verapamil and Emgality—is still essential.
What I’ve learned from this journey is that it’s not one single solution—it’s a combination. Preventive therapy keeps things stable. Acute treatments like oxygen and triptans provide backup when needed. And for me, psilocybin appeared to interrupt the cycle in a way nothing else had.
Today, my life is very different. I’m no longer trapped by my condition the way I once was. I still respect it, I still prepare for it, but I have tools that work—and that has given me a level of control I didn’t think was possible before.
This is my personal experience. It’s not a recommendation or a guideline, but it is
topic: My Journey with Episodic Cluster Headaches and Psilocybin Intervention Adam Estroff – Parkland, Florida

5-Meo-DALT experience
I have been a chronic cluster headache sufferer since 2021.
Each year the condition has gotten worse and more frequent.
In 2023 I started my journey with the typical GP referral to neurology. Multiple diagnosis and failed medical trials later my condition really deteriorated in 2024. I was not having multiple attacks a day with little breaks between bouts.
Medical interventions.
Indomethacin - no effect
Lamotrigine ( I reacted extremely bad to this and seen a spike in attacks almost doubling and continuing like this for months)
Verapamil - suffered brachacardia so never made it to effective dosing
Atogepant - some partial relief from shadows
GON block - 1st one gave me 3 weeks the second one failed to have any effect
Multi cranial nerve block - both times gave me 5 weeks freedom
gamma core - no effect
Mitchell D. Post - Cluster Headache Patient Survey - 5-MeO-DALT.pdf
Alternative therapy
MM - months of trying to bust using 1 - 1.8g every 5 days to little effect.
DMT pen - very effective at halting attacks for me within around 1-2 minutes.
Around September of 2025 I read the case study published by Jonathon Leighton from a recent study on psychedelics and CH.
I was 4 weeks post MCNB and knew it was only a matter of time before the beast would return.
Jonathon was kind enough to go into some more details and he sent me the document on 5-Meo-dalt which I had never even heard of. I figured it was worth a try and managed to get some sent to my friend’s mums house in Ireland (where it is legal) and then forward on to me in Scotland.
For me it was life changing. By the time I got the substance my attacks had indeed returned and I was back into a state of crisis having multiple 7-8-9/10 attacks a week and sometimes am and pm. I followed the protocol on the document and my first dose was on the 13th of September 2025. I kept dosing every 5 - 7 days and it took my attacks down to shadow like pain. My first real “attack” would be on the 30th of October and was around a 7/10 but only lasted 20 minutes. Fast forward now and the brief overview is 5MD took me out of the crisis state of daily excruciating attacks to the odd painful attack that was short lasting. I still suffered shadows on and off weekly but I had lots of days that I was pain free. I hadn’t lived like this for around 1 year.
I am still so thankful for Jonathon for sharing the document and introducing me to 5MD. It’s not perfect and I’m still on my search for a full remission but I’m living life.
This month I decided to take a break from 5MD and try LSD to see if I can get any closer to remission. If not I will go back to the 5MD. The beauty of this is it’s so mild at the doses mentioned on the case study (25 - 15mg) the experience is super mild. At 15mg I am barely aware of it so as a dad and business owner this really fits my hectic schedule.
Feel free to dm me or ask questions here. Im happy to share more of my experience and hopefully it can be of some use for others.

Hello Everyone,
I’ve been an episodic cluster headache sufferer for nearly twenty years now. I am father of three with a fairly stressful job in the tech industry. I usually get a flare up of cluster headaches every 12 to 18 months. They typically last 3 months.
Last winter, the headaches were starting to come on, and I did 4 doses of magic mushrooms one week apart (around 1 gram each time). It did the trick and I experienced nearly four months of completely pain free existence, not even a hint of shadows, which is very unusual for me.
Unfortunately in the spring the headaches came back. I only had 1 gram of mushrooms left and it wasn’t enough. I never experienced a flare up in the spring so I thought I was safe for a while. I was about to make another batch… Lesson learned.
I’m now 3 and a half months into this flare up. It is starting to cool off finally. In the middle of the summer I was at 800 mg of verapamil. The side effects weren’t pleasant. Now I’m down to 480. I plan on going fully verapamil free in the next three weeks. Five days after being off verapamil, I plan on taking 2 grams of mushroom as a precaution, and repeat the dose every two or three weeks for the foreseeable future.
I want to thank Bob Wold and the people behind cluster busters. The site and forums is an invaluable resource for cluster headaches sufferers. Over the past several years I visited the site over a dozen times to seek out information. Sometimes I won’t visit cluster busters for years (perhaps as a way to compartmentalize this terrible condition) only to come back when the headaches return.
I have several questions, mostly about drugs interactions when busting. I’ve posted a few of these in the past. And I’ve search the forums for answers, but there is so many search results that it can be difficult to find out the most agreed upon answer.
Is Benadryl safe when busting?
There seems to be conflicting anecdotes in the forums. Is there any consensus on this point? I’ve noticed that it helps with nighttime shadows/attacks. Should I clear my system of it for five days first?
Does CBD oil affect the busting process?
I’m curious about the effects of CBD oil on reducing shadows. I haven’t tried it yet. I’ll wait until after
busting, but I’m curious if there are any negative consequences when busting.
Are high doses of melatonin safe when busting?
I’m currently taking 18mg. I usually lower it to 3 mg when I’m not experiencing cluster headaches but I’m afraid of lowering until after the bust. Melatonin really helps my nocturnal attacks and shadows.
Is anybody else greatly affected by Sunlight?
The hardest aspect of being a cluster headache sufferer is that I’m basically allergic to sunlight. Hi forum, very glad to find this website!
After reading the various stories, I feel like many were like my own situation. So here is my story...very long and not especially extraordinary, but I thought to include all details in case it helps any other newbie someday (e.g. hey that is just like me). So sorry for the length of post.
I started experiencing clusters when I was 18yr in my first yr university, in spring (March-April) several times a day lasting for 4-6weeks. Unbelievable pain (always right side, started with shadows as I now know them to be called, then jaw and neck pain, then spreading to eye socket, felt like a tapered wooden peg, the kind to kill a vampire with,  being hammered in to the eye socket and out through back of skull, very bad), I took OTC pain meds (tylenol overlap with ibuprofen) but did nothing. Saw family doc, thought it was allergies, sent me to dentist but nothing there, sent me to optometrist (since I mentioned eye pain) and got crappy very low prescription. By that time, the cluster episode was over.
Then I did not have another for a few years, then came back again with same pattern and same thing (tried OTC paid meds, saw family doc, blah blah, run-around until cluster over. Then forgot about until next one, again 3yrs later again in the spring, very painful and was done university and working outdoors on the road travelling doing environmental tests out in the field, so could not call in sick, suffered through for the episode of 4-6 weeks. (Note: for the theory of low testosterone and low vit D...I am not sure about, I mean I was outside in the sun almost always during this work for 15hrs per day, and I was in my early 20s working out in gym like crazy, really good libido, so think my vit D and testosterone was fine, but was getting crappy sleep so totally could buy the upset biological clock connection)
Same pattern with episodes every 2-3yrs typically in spring for 4-6weeks with max frequency/day in middle of cluster, just suffered through and probably almost OD'ing on OTC pain meds which did almost nothing.
Then in my mid 30's when I was on parental leave for my first child and was the only care provider at home, when a cluster epodes started. I knew I could not watch this baby with multiple attacks daily, so went to ER hoping they could figure out. This is in Ottawa, Canada, I went to a larger hospital which is part of University of Ottawa med program, so very good competent people up to date on everything. I had done some homework by now and had suspected my headaches were CH. text:
Hello all.  I wanted to share a little of my story with clusters.  I should have probably posted this a little while back, but its hard to sometimes relive all of it. I apologize for how long this is.  I should work on a cliff notes version.
Everything started in January of 2014.  I started waking up during sleep every hour or two with the most intense pain i had ever felt in and around my right eye.  The pain shot through my head and went down the right side of my neck and last about 30 minutes to an hour.  At first I figured I must be sleeping wrong.  First I tried new pillows, but nothing changed.  Then I thought maybe a chiropractor could help, but was of course wrong.  The pain kept hitting me every night, and consistently every 2 hours after I went to sleep.  Finally went to a doctor after a month.  Up to this point the headaches only came on at night.  During the day everything was fine except me being a little exhausted.  The doctor didn't really have any ideas and wanted me to go to physical therapy.  I knew he was wrong and never did go.  Started looking for a new doctor after that.
The new doctor was much more helpful, but still didn't have any ideas.  Then one night we had a breakthrough that helped pinpoint the problem.  This is something that is hard to explain.  One night shortly after my first visit with the new doctor I was in the kitchen trying to ride one of the headaches out.  There was a voice in my head.  It just said take the medicine in the junk drawer.  My wife used to suffer from migraines, but they stopped about 6 months before my clusters.  Unknown to me one of her old maxalt tabs was in that drawer.  I took it and the headache melted away within 5 minutes.  It never came back that night.
With that new information and a little googling my wife and I came across cluster headaches.  All the symptoms lined up exactly with what I had been dealing with.  On the next visit I explained all this to the new doc.  She said she had also done some research and wanted to talk to me about cluster headaches.  This was when I was given my first prescription for verapamil.
The verapamil helped, but about this time my clusters also changed.  Instead of just getting them at night I started getting them during the day.  I also started experiencing my first shadows about this time.  Headaches were always about the same time.  The cycles would drift some.  Looser schedule and less intense, but slowly becoming fixed at the same times each day and much more intense.  Then the cycle would fade and start over.  During this time I was getting the headaches pretty much every day.  There was the occasional random day where I was pain free, but it was rare.
Things continued like this until October of that first year.  I dealt with the pain and headaches as much as possible and was using Maxalt as an abortive.  I also had access to some shots that were left from my wife's issues with migraines.  In the middle of October things shot up to 11.  I had

Hello everyone, this is my first post as I am new to the community. I’ll first share my story trying to be brief (I won’t/can't).
I’ve separated my post in a first presentation part, and in a second part where i talk about my recent busting experience. English is not my native language but don't hesitate to correct me if I am gramatically wrong.
My name is Serge, 32 years old, from Geneva in Switzerland. I am born from a Romanian father and a Guatemalan mother. I’ve been in couple for more than 10 years and married for almost 5. I have two boys 5 and 6 years old. I’ve had headaches from as long as I could remember, but i couldn’t identify patterns. They were just so bad and no doctor was able to understand it.
My cluster journey really started in september 2018, we went to spain in vacation and everyday I got terrible headaches. When we got back home, they were becoming worse, showing up at night, at work, it started having a really bad impact on my life. After two years of suffering almost everyday, i wasn’t strong enough to keep my job.
I was a gate and check-in supervisor at the local airport, I had a good carreer there on the rise, but it came to a stop. In this job, you need to be fully available, you work early and late, you can't be absent even for a few minutes if you're in pain, so it wasnt compatible with my shit condition.
I’ve kept trying to go back to work after some resting periods, and it never worked, the intense stress, hard schedules, made my pain worsening when back at work.
When you are on sick leave for more than 3 months, the swiss law requires people to apply for disability. therefore, during a very long process, I’ve been sent to make some medical and psychologicyl exams to definite if I can still work or not.
It was around the same time that a proper cluster headache diagnosis had been made. Before this i was suggested (and convinced) that I had arnold neuralgia, I’ve already had tried all the numerous migraine medications you know.I had already tried nerve decompression in the back of the head too, with no results of course.
In late 2020 the examinations i underwent concluded that i had borderline personality disorders, and confirmed the cluster headache diagnosis.
At this moment when I had this annoucement I was trying to work again at the airport, but it didn’t went well, my wife and I lost our 5 weeks old daughter and that sent me into an even deeper depression, leading to the end of my contract.
The state soon declared me 100% invalid. In Switzerland when this happens, your salary is paid by retirment office and by occupational pension. Because i have children, I get monthly roughly the same amount of money that when I used to work my 40 weekly hours.
It took time to reconstruct, but at the same time I met a better neurologist (the one that properly diagnosed me) that prescibed me isoptin, oxygen and cymbalta, and as triptans, zomig 5mg nasal spray. At first and until recently it worked pretty well, almost all pain a

Hi fellow sufferers,
Two months ago I was diagnosed with cluster headaches. I’m 29 years old. It started in mid-November. At first the attacks were “moderate” and always came at 10 a.m. After about a week, the intensity increased. The attacks became more severe and now came at 10 a.m. and 3 p.m., usually lasting 1–2 hours.
I felt it in my temple, my jaw, above my eye, below my eye, in my nose, at the back of my head, and on my scalp. It felt like someone was hammering a huge nail from top to bottom straight through my face. I could even feel it in my palate. I get goosebumps just thinking about it. I thought I was going to die or had a brain tumor or something similar. At first there were only the attacks, and afterwards I was pain-free again.
After 5–6 severe attacks, a one-sided constant pain above my eye set in, which lasted 24/7, and the attacks seemed to arise out of that. The pain first brought me to my family doctor, who couldn’t help and sent me to the hospital after three days with no improvement. At that point I was taking ibuprofen like candy with no effect. I still kept taking it because I thought: at some point it has to work.
At the hospital I described my symptoms to the neurologist: a blocked nose, a watery eye, and head and facial pain that I didn’t think a human being could have and survive. She immediately recognized it: cluster headaches. I was then treated with triptan injections. The attacks were stopped, but the constant headache remained.
I had never heard of this disease before and started reading about it while still in the hospital. I used to be a smoker and quit the same day. Later I was treated at a pain clinic that had experience with cluster headaches. I kept a pain diary and read more and more about it. I started supplementing D3K2. And I found out that bright light triggers the attacks. The intensity of the attacks became somewhat lower—until they finally disappeared after about two weeks. However, the constant headache remained and I began to develop very intense light sensitivity. From then on, a baseball cap and sunglasses were my best friends.
I remembered that I had something similar about three years ago, though not with this intensity of attacks. Back then they thought I had sinusitis and I thought my wisdom teeth were simply causing problems. It was already intense enough at the time that they did a CT scan and found nothing. But it wasn’t comparable to the attacks now, and back then I didn’t even realize they were “attacks.” But I do remember the same constant headache above the eye that lasted for weeks and then simply disappeared from one day to the next. That’s when it became clear to me that it had already been cluster back then, knocking for the first time.
To get rid of the constant headache and end the episode for good, the doctor at the pain clinic prescribed prednisolone. He said normally they would also add verapamil, but since the attacks were largely over, it wasn’t necessary (thank God). In t

Hello everyone,
First time posting. I am so glad I found this site and have gotten to read so many stories similar to mine. ItÂ’s good to know that I am not alone and there is a group of well informed/supportive people out there. I also love the fact that people have found alternative methods for curing/relieving the pain. Anyhow, IÂ’m not sure if I am posting this in the right place but I feel the need to share it.
This year started off as one of the worst years of my life. Sure, I had been getting these episodes for over 10 years, but it had been almost two years without any and now the attacks were back and more frequent and intense then ever (one to two attacks every day for nearly 5 months).
I had seen my doctor about the attacks in the past but was always told they were just migraines or tension headaches. She told me to take some OTC meds and was once given a migraine prescription med, which did nothing. Due to the meds really having no effect, and because I was always reluctant to take pharmaceutical drugs, most of the time I would just Â“tough it outÂ”. After trying the tough it method during the episode early this year, I had finally reached my breaking point. The CH came for a 5th day in a row while I was at work. I told my boss that I couldnÂ’t work like this any longer and that I would be taking time off to pursue treatment of this condition. I walked out of work that day and immediately called my doctor.
Deep down I knew what the condition was but I was too afraid to admit it. I had done research a few years back and my symptoms aligned with the condition known as Cluster Headaches. I was in denial at the time and never told anyone what I thought it was. From what I read, there was no magic pill to cure it, so I did not want to admit to myself that I had an incurable condition. I simply tried to ignore it.
However, on that day I went to my doctorÂ’s office and decided it was time to confront the beast. I told my doctor they were not migraines but were Cluster Headaches. She said she didnÂ’t know much about it and then referred me to a neurologist. The neurologist said it sounded possible and after he left the room for 10 minutes he came back and confirmed it was CH. Finally, it was like a weight lifted off my shoulders. It was a day a dreaded but finally putting a name to the horrible condition gave me some sore of relief.
I began a treatment plan that consisted of prednisone, verapamil, and imitrex. Every time time I took the imitrex, I couldnÂ’t breathe and it felt like my chest was closing in. Overall, the side effects from all the drugs had me feeling like a zombie and I was still getting the attacks. After a month without any relief I went back to the Doc and told him about the side effects, and he simply gave me different abortive medication and upped the verapamil dosage. I have not posted on this site before, but have been skulking around it for years and I figure I may as well add our story because without so many of you (who don't even know it!) things would be so much different!
My husband is an episodic cluster sufferer.  Started in 1995 and came on hard and fast.  "Suicide headache" is about the only appropriate term for what he would suffer.  (And I loved the GP whom I tried to explain this to who suggested my husband might need to be checked into a facility if he was throwing around terms like suicide.)  My husband would get headaches every 6 or 12 or 18 months and the doctors didn't diagnose it for years.  It was a TERRIBLE time for him.
It took years before we got the cluster diagnosis and finally found Prednisone which ... kind of ... helped.  The steroid made him crazy.  Literally.  Aggressive, impatient, angry and mad which are polar opposites of his character.  Also, many times, the clusters would 'break through' when he weaned down to 10mg (after starting at 70 or 80!) and he would have to start the prednisone all over again.
10 years ago he found oxygen and that was a godsend.  Together with Imitrex and handfuls of whatever other pills were in the house, I could keep him vaguely able to handle the intense amount of pain that gripped him.  It was especially fun when our son was born and he was hit with the worst cluster ever (Spring 2003) and couldn't tolerate any noise at all.
We continued at this pace until 2008 when he was hit in March.  Prednisone allowed breakthroughs FOUR times and he was still having headaches in August.  The doctors (GP and Neuro) kept prescribing ever increasing doses of Prednisone - together with Topamax (migraines) and Verapamil (tension headaches) which had never done him any good.  One night when I was about at the end of my tether, I found this website - and it changed everything.
We did the cleansing, hopped on the Vitamin M trail, and never looked back!  Every three months kept them at bay the first year.  Every four months and nothing the second year!  Really, it was like a miracle!!  It has been hard for me not to stand on a soap box and shout this out to everyone I meet - but instead - we remain quietly at home, trying to keep it on the down low.  He never saw his neurologist after that because he didn't want to have this kind of thing in his medical file.
Enter Thanksgiving this year.  We had grown complacent and moved to a six month maintenance schedule for October.  We hit mid month and he realized he had a tiny bit of Vitamin M left.  He took it - but it had no effect.  I literally WATCHED the headaches come over the next few days.  His right eye will begin to droop just a bit and then grow more and more flat.  We only have one place to procure our vitamins and there was a major miscommunication.
The headache hit on November 29th.  It was such a crushing blow - and SO our own fault.  Wednesday morning my husband called the GP and the neurologist to ask for his

Hi,
My name is Ton, I live in Amsterdam. I have been having cluster headaches for about 15+ years I guess, but I only started getting proper 'cluster periods' about 8 years ago. ItÂ  was always relatively mild, till about 4 years ago, when I started seeing doctor about it. Had some tests etc and finally was sent to a neurologist who diagnosed CH. The final diagnosis was at the beginning of a cluster 2 years ago.
My neurologist advised verapamil, and sumatriptan for acute treatment. Near the end of the cluster I was taking 960 mg of verapamil a day (which I have been told is a ridiculous amount) and almos every night the maximum allowed 2 injections of sumatriptan. My cluster subsided then, but it had been going on for months so there's a good chance it had nothing to do with the verapamil. In fact, this medically treated cluster was the WORST THING EVER. Lasted a lot longer than previous clusters, my headaches were way worse, and for the first time I started having attacks during the day, and bad ones. Oxygen helped a bit, it shortened the attacks but rarely really aborted the bad ones. This was not a route I wanted to go down again.
So when I noticed a nasty headache a month ago I immediately went online and ordered a (cubensis) mushroom grow kit (fyi: it is not legal to sell magic mushrooms in Holland any more but growkits are legal). By the time my mushrooms were ready I was in full cluster, and I took my first dose 2 weeks ago. I had never actually tried magic mushrooms before, so I decided what the heck, if you're gonna do it, at least have some fun :-) So together with a friend who had some experience I made tea (the recipe with lemon juice/vitamine C/instant coffee) and we had a fun evening. I took about 2.5 grams of dried mushroom and had what I guess was a mild trip (I'm a big guy, 115 kilo. my 75 kilo friend who took the same dose experienced a lot more), lots of bad humor etc. And that night I slept quite well, only had a mild headache in the morning. The next 4 nights I had headaches, but a lot milder than the week before, then I had two pretty bad nights. As I wanted to time my intake with the weekend I waited till friday again before taking my next dose, 2 grams as I want to find the point where it is effective without having a full on trip. Still quite dizzy, and had some headaches that night, and the following days, worse than after I took the first dose. This wednesday (decided to do the 5 day thing) took 1.5 grams (did not add the instant coffee to the tea mixture this time) and to my surprise I felt more out of it that the last time with 2 grams. Could be a difference in theÂ  mushrooms, the first batch I dried on a heater, which I read later may have hurt the potency. Had a bad night, and thursday was quite shadowy. But thursday my oxygen arrived, so last night whenever I woke up with the beginning of a headache I hit the oxygen and was fine in a couple of minutes, did not even get close to a 'real' headache. I am 35 years old. Suffering from cluster headaches since I was 19. I don't even really know where to start with this.  I stumbled onto this sight last week and I have seen my story in different variations at different times in my life.  Every time I seem to have found a solution that is gonna last it crumbles. I am a episodic Cher as you guys say.  May to August...or at least it used to be. As a youth I would just suffer through the summer. The cycle was shorter. June and August. Hi guys, I have been familiar with this website for a couple years now. But this is my first time posting, so I guess I'll give a bit of a backdrop. text:
I'm going to try and include everything that I have gone through up until this point ever since my headaches started 3.5 yrs ago but the list is long
It started on 1/31/2015 when I had Lasik eye surgery and within a week I started experiencing extreme eye pain afterwards. At this point, I was convinced the problems had to do with my eyes so I continued following up with my Lasik surgeon. Do yourself a favor if you ever get Lasik. First, don't use Groupon for Lasik eye surgery. Trust me, spend the extra dollar on it, your health is worth it. Second, always always always google your surgeon for previous lawsuits. I did not follow either of these lessons and ended up with a quack of a doctor who refused to accept any blame for the pain I soon experienced thereafter. It was initially determined that the cause of my headaches was because my vision was over corrected, essentially straining my eyes working in front of the computer screen 8+ hours a day contributing to these headaches. I mean, it did makes sense at the time. It was at this point that I found a 2nd Lasik doctor I trusted to perform an additional Lasik procedure  to "make my vision worse" in order to reduce the effects of the eye strain of the first procedure. At this point I had only visited my primary care physician and had been eating ibuprofen and Excedrin like candy to get through the day. It got to the point where I was taking 16 Excedrin each work day in order to function.
The following prescriptions during those first 7 months were as follows:
Vicodin 5-300 mg
gatifloxacin 0.5 % ophthalmic drops
Prolensa 0.07 % ophthalmic drops
sumatriptan succinate 100 mg
azithromycin 250 mg oral tablet
propranolol 80 mg oral tablet
hydrocodone-acetaminophen 10-325 mg
acetaminophen-codeine 300-30 mg
butalbital-acetaminophen-caff 50-325-40 mg
prednisolone acetate 1 % ophthalmic drops,suspension
As I'm sure most of you are also experts on headache prescriptions as well, nothing here really stands out as a great headache medication, but i hadn't seen a neurologist yet either. So I have my second Lasik procedure and after some time to heal my headaches are still there. By the way, my headaches never go away. They literally never have not stopped since that initial surgery 1/31/2015 until the very second I'm typing this post right now. I've taken medication to dull the pain, but to this day, I have yet to be pain free. Anyway, I seek one last treatment regarding the eyes by flying to Florida from Indy to visit an "Ophthalmologist" to create custom $5k scleral lenses which were supposed to cure my headaches but I'm pretty sure that doctor was even worse than my initial Lasik doctor. It was now time to start seeing neurologists.
I will go ahead and speed through the part about visiting all the neurologists and just go ahead and list everything that I have tried in the subsequent two years up until about a month ago. I started through the Diamond headache clinic in Chicago and got some valuable informatio

I had my first headache at age 23 in summer of 2008 a day after visiting the dentist. The pain was centered around my left eye and on the same side of my head as my recent dental work. It didn't last long however I immediately suspected some kind inflammation due to dental work and called my dentist. I emphasized the extreme level of pain and she prescribed me percocet. The next headache came a few days later and I immediately took a percocet. By the time it kicked it, the headache was over. Around this time I began to notice some the shadows of headaches about 10-25 minutes before they hit full force. My affected side nostril would clog up and soon after, I would get a headache. The next few weeks I spent playing a cat and mouse game of trying to time taking percocet to conquer them. Around this time I noticed that roughly 3pm was "headache time" and I anticipated about 30-60 minutes of hell around then. Ultimately I was never able to suppress the headaches using percocet and around the time I ran out, the headaches mysteriously stopped. I soon forgot about them and moved on with my life.
Roughly a year later, severe headaches started up again and this time the pain was much worse however the frequency less. I went to the doctor who believed I had migraines. He prescribed me a migraine medicine (I forgot which one) that did absolutely nothing. A year after that, I got them again and sought no treatment. I began telling everyone that late summer/fall was my "headache season" and during this time I might have to step away from dinners/work to seclude myself when they occur. The pain seemed to get worse every year and my incidental reports to doctors including a neurologist were met with non-action. In 2013, I decided the stuffed nose indicated the headaches were caused by sinus pressure and started using nasal spray to keep it clear. It probably didn't help however at the time I thought it did.
Finally in 2014, the headaches coupled with other neurological symptoms (pain in my fingertips that could be "cured" by dipping them in ice water) spurned me to find a new doctor. She was very concerned and remarked "My first thought is that you have a brain tumor, but you'd be dead. My next thought is that you have MS however you don't look like someone who has MS". This frightened me and I immediately sought out a new neurologist and talked to an ENT. MRI of my cervical spine revealed herniated discs which my neuro thought caused finger pain. The ENT ordered an MRI of my brain which found "3 tiny hyperintense FLAIR foci in the subcortical white matter". Everything else was normal. My neurologist told me that FLAIR foci are normal in older people (I was 29 at the time) and headache patients and not to be concerned (though I am still concerned). After doing some research, I thought cluster headaches could be possible. A different neurologist aggressively shot down my theory and told me that I just had migraines. My headache season seemed to extend longer in

hi everyone,
i'm just getting online here and wanted to introduce myself and ask some advice.Â  i live in southern argentina (in the patagonian andes), though i was born and raised in the US, i've been a transplant for around 9 years now.Â
to give a reasonably complete history may take some time, so if you don't want to read more but might be able to answer my straight-up question, here goes:
if the only thing i've been taking is sumatriptan pills (not on any other drug for abortion or prevention), how necessary is it to wait the whole 5 days detox before busting with LSD (it's been 2.5 so far)?Â
but i see people often ask for more details, which seems like a good idea so i'll try to be reasonably thorough... apologies for length.Â  i've suffered from cluster headaches for just over a decade, though i didn't know that's what they were until about 2 years ago (went thru the normal run-around i bet you're all familiar with, being told it was all kinds of other things and trying unsuccessful remedies).
while visiting family in the US, i had another cycle, a great doctor finally pegged it and told me i have CH...Â  Â he prescribed imitrex and verapamil.Â  imitrex worked perfectly to abort, but i can't say the verapamil did much to break the cycle earlier than usual.
a year ago i had my next cycle (back down south here), again took imitrex to abort attacks, but no verapamil, and as usual after 3 weeks the cycle stopped of its own accord.
in general i tend to get hit once every other day, like clockwork in late morning.  though sometimes it hits on consecutive days or lets 2 days pass between attacks.Â  an untreated attack lasts 3-5 hours.Â  now that i'm reading some of your stories i gather i've been lucky, some of you have much higher frequency.
now i've entered another cycle, have been taking imigran (sumatriptan like imitrex) and things are going funny.Â  it still aborts attacks, but after 7-10 days the attacks started to get closer together.Â  from every two days to every day, to every 12 hours, to every 8 or 9 hours.Â  and sometimes, depending on how quickly i get medicated, i still have to suffer through an hour or so before it takes effect.Â  attacks also tend to come on slower, so instead of taking 10 minutes to ramp up to full power (kip8 or 9, with occasional bursts of 10) it may take 30-40.
so i really want to break this cycle and if possible stop using the sumatriptan, since i imagine it's what's causing these increases in attacks (as documented on this website)... and aside from being expensive, it's hard to get-- for example i had several doses going into the weekend, but since the frequency stepped up i ran out way quicker than expected, and the pharmacy has to special order it (remember, i live in patagonia)... and just this morning they said the order came back empty because even the central distributor is out.
so two days ago i tried oxygen for the first time since i happened to be in town when it hit... just walked into the hospital

16 hours ago, Bejeeber said:
Agreed with FunTimes about a steroid taper being a way to get you some attack free days (also agreed with Snafu about the D3 and O2 if you can swing it).
@CHfather
asked about verapamil previously - it is commonly prescribed at a relatively high dose at the same time as the prednisone (steroid), allowing it to gradually take preventive effect while the pred fills the gap. Tried that approach yet? If you get on a telemed call again, that could be something to inquire about, maybe along with an RX for plenty of triptan sprays or injections for breakthrough attacks. I can imagine how verap might be tricky with telemed though, as it affects your blood pressure (lowers it), etc., and a doctor may feel more comfy prescribing it if they can do an actual physical exam?
One thing about the triptan sprays or injections is they will often keep attacks at bay for more like 3-4 hours at a time when DMT or O2 offers much shorter lasting relief. Injections
may
be more affordable than sprays if the 6mg dose is split/reduced by you to 2mg. Warning though: Triptans (and possibly some other pharma meds)
can
cause what would have otherwise been something like an 8 week bout to extend longer.
Occasionally Gabapentin will be a semi-effective preventive for a CH'er, but especially in this case I DO NOT RECOMMEND, as it comes with a serious side effect for us of triggering suicidal ideation.

Next diagnose was beginning arthritis in the jaw joint.... I was like "you got to be kidding me, I'm 20 years old. I bite my teeth together because of the pain". Again not the cause but the effect. Years went by, got an episode around 1,5-2,5 years apart during spring or fall. Starting with only one attack at 4am. Ten years in I got my first doctor to describe me meds for CH and sent me to neurologist. My episode was long gone before I got there and it didn't help any since I already knew what I have. Basically when I got the meds to treat this I had used all the painkillers, muscle relaxants and opioids there is. Been eating verapamil and prednisone for years every time horton rises it's head, year by year they are coming more often and harder. Imigran injections, and sumatriptan tablets when I'm broke (always lol). Every time I figure out its pattern it changes. Once I've had it change sides, my god I was totally lost! And of course my doctor said it can't be CH if it changes sides!! Pffft
Been through two pregnancys with CH, had it actually active when I went to labour with the second one. Had to go through the last moth of pregnancy without anything else but paracetamol, the doctor wouldn't prescribe me O2 because she didn't know how that would affect the featus... Neadles to say I was pissed and tired....
Anyhow I think the time I got the "right" meds for this the episodes started to go worse. Every episode we tried something else with the normal preventatives. And every time it worked at first and the next time mr. Horton had found a way to go through it.
Now I'm three weeks in to my episode, first week was easy, second week bad then it started to go every two hours for an hour and the shadows didn't go away in the middle for a second.
