I've not been on any CH support sites for a long time. Things had been going good the last few years. Right now...well...yeah.
I've been fighting chronic CH for 19 years now. Yep, 19 years. I've been through a hell of a lot with them, close to suicide more than once. It's a bitch. I'm fortunate to have an understanding employer and a caring wife. I don't know how I could have survived over the years without the support.
I've been seeing Dr. Sachdeva in San Ramon for years, she's probably a lot of the reason I've survived this long. On top of that, she got me over to Dr. Goadsby when he was at UCSF getting their headache clinic up and running. You know your CH sucks when Dr. Goadsby tells you that you're one of his toughest cases. Unfortunately, Dr. Goadsby has moved on to other places to help other people, and the doctor at UCSF who took over CH cases since then was doing well, but he has now moved on as well, leaving those of us with CH on a waiting list.
Over a decade ago, I spent five days sitting in UCSF hospital with a tube in my arm getting DHE dosed every few hours. After that, I was titrated up on Verapamil to where things finally worked to calm my CH down, which was at 320mg 3X a day (yep, 960mg a day). I did reasonably well on it, the CH was there at a low enough level I could survive and when it started getting bad they would do a greater occipital nerve block that would calm things down for a while. Over the last few years, my heart started slowing down (thanks to hitting middle age) and I had to get weaned down on the Verapamil to 160mg 3X a day based on the EKG and review with the cardiologist I see (he's Dr. Sachdeva's husband, so they both keep an eye on me!). I've done another three visits in to UCSF for five days of DHE to quiet things down as well since then.
So now I've got my daily Verapamil, 200IU of D3 nightly, and Duloxitine for the depression that always comes with chronic pain. I use Tylenol for lower level hits, then Imitrex for the harder ones. Oxygen therapy has unfortunately never worked for me (Dr. Goadsby said sometimes that's just how it goes). When it gets really insane, my wife will run me off to the ER and they'll throw a combination of stuff into me, fortunately that happens only once every few months. The last year had been quiet for me, I think because my body was preoccupied dealing with pain from shattering a heel when I fell off a ladder. Two surgeries later, I still get a lot of pain from it, but it's down to a point that the CH is now taking over my life again.
It's autumn again, and this is the time that the beast always gets nasty with me. I think it has to do with the rapidly changing amount of light during the day (autumn and spring both wreak havoc on me) affecting circadian rhythm. I tried getting in touch with UCSF, but there's no one there right now seeing the CH patients. Dr. Sachdeva is off on vacation for the next couple weeks as well. I'd really love to go spend a week having DHE pumped into

Hi everyoneÂ… My name is Todd, time to start my first thread here.
I havenÂ’t wanted to post on this because as we all know the beast is unpredictable, and I do not want to scare anyone off of using triptans because they obviously work in terms of getting rid of the pain, and it just may be I am going chronic.
Here it isÂ… I have the distinct feeling triptans are dragging out my cycles, and the nature of my HA has changed since I began relying on them. O2 has almost always worked to clear a hit completely. I use a steamy bathroom and mirror metaphor for thisÂ… The bathroom my head, the steam the beast, the mirror is my conscious window on life.
As far as daytime is concerned, the usual hits at 10am and 2pm, O2 used to completely clear the steam from the roomÂ… clear clean and ready to rock. Now I am finding O2 just rubs a little clear hole on the mirror, and the steamy bathroom just fogs it up again in a short time (20 minutes to an hour)Â… never clear and clean.
Nighttime is different. Something about the slumber/sleep state triggers hits for me. I usually get an hour at most lying down before waking in agony, and that continues as long as I am trying to sleep. O2 aborts fine, and I would stay clear as long as I donÂ’t try to go back to sleep. Basically, if I donÂ’t want to get hit, I donÂ’t even lay down. My pre-triptan sleep cocktail evolved from trying to pry that hour window open to 5 or 6 hours to get sleep. But that cocktail was pretty heavy, taking a few aborts in the morning before returning to the usual 10 and 2 hits but otherwise clear day. So, I started taking amerge (naratriptan), then switched to relpax (eletriptan) for sleep and getting 6-8 hours pain freeÂ… but at what cost I am wondering.
I am a 36 year veteran of CH, and for 30+ years I never had a cycle last more than 4 months, usually 3 or less. Now I am in foggy bathroom hell with cycles that are 6-8 months. I am used to cycles in spring and fall, with never a hint of CH in between. Now I am getting incomplete remissionÂ… shadows and night hits. Maybe I just have that Â“need to blame it on somethingÂ”.

As for my remissionsÂ… they aren't what they used to be. I have always had a problem sleeping, always had a little nervous energy problem. But now no sleep at all unless I knock myself out with meds. text:
Hello, I’ve been getting cluster headaches for probably 20 years now.  I only get them maybe once (for 1-2 months) every two years usually when the seasons change.  I usually get about 3 headaches a day during this period.
I had a doctor I saw in Florida and the main treatments was prednisone, verapamil, and then an abortive like sumatriptan.  They also prescribed high flow oxygen.   They also prescribed anti-convulsants but I never tried it..  I guess I was scared of the side effects.  Usually by the time I  get all the meds and regimen going, my cluster cycle ends.
Fast forward to now, I now live in Japan where it’s harder to get a high flow oxygen due to limited resources.  So, I have some meds I saved (from 2 years ago)..  I started 50mg prednisone, 80mg Verapamil per day, and that keeps the pain down but I still end up using about 50mg of Sumatriptan per day.   I have the 20mg nasal spray for emergencies as well.
My current problem is that I’ve been on 50mg of prednisone per day for over a month now and I hate taking prednisone.  My skin is also breaking out and I’m not sure if it’s due to the prednisone doing something to my body or if the breakout (heat rash or something) is from taking super hot showers to help with the pain of the cluster headache?
When I anticipate an attack, I usually take 40mg Verapamil, and 30mg of prednisone, then I go to the 24 hour gym (very painful drive usually at 3am) and I either run for an hour or do the stair stepper (with deep breathing exercise's) for an hour or until the pain is manageable.   I usually end up jumping in the shower several times as well because the hot water helps to distract the pain I guess.  During the attack, It feels like there’s a fog in my head and focusing and waking up will help with the pain.
When an attack happens, I feel a nerve running down the left side of my head/neck and into my upper back is cramping.  I can feel this nerve run from my upper left side back into my neck and then up into the left side of my cranium and that’s where the intense throbbing pain is.
As most of you know, REM sleep can trigger these headaches so I often wake up out of a super deep sleep with a headache and an intense urge to run and get oxygen into my body.  I also try stretching the muscles in my neck but I’m not sure if that helps or aggravates the headache.  It feels like if I sleep on the left side of my head, my chances for waking up with a headache are increased?
I just now started reading about the Vitamin D3 regimen and I’ve started taking the Vitamin D3, but still have to purchase the rest of the regimen.   I really feel like I must start tapering off of the prednisone, and I need to stop the sumatriptan due to the rebound effect.
I’m flying back to the US this weekend for four days because I have some shrooms I want to take to see if that will stop my cycle.  I’m desperate to stop this a cycle because it’s debilitating.
I’m posting this just to see if anyone has any good suggestions.  Si

Hello All,
Long time episodic sufferer (over 32 years, about 20 cycles) who's been getting by in recent cycles with Verapamil and O2. Thought I would try the D3 regimen instead of Verapamil, it has seemed to help in the early weeks along with O2, of course, but week 3 & 4 the intensity of the every 1-2 hour nightly headaches was picking up and I thought I would try busting.
Living in Bulgaria so my options are limited but I was able to get some dried MM. I took one of the tiniest mushrooms in the jar (no way to weigh them, was told they were strong) a week ago Sunday. I could certainly feel them, especially hour 2&3. I was glad at the time that I didn't take more since I read you don't really need strong doses to stop CH. Didn't notice much help though week 4 is always a nasty part of my typical cycle.
I read about waiting 5 days but I couldn't find any rational why other than maybe waiting 5 days after stopping other meds that might interfere. Since I'm not taking meds, only the D3 regimen, I got impatient and tried again on Wednesday, 3 days later. I thought I would try a slightly stonger dose so I took what I thought was 1 1/2  times the first dose. I felt no rush at all in this dose, actually got sleepy, went to bed early and was headache free all night.
Unfortunately the next 3 nights were my worst so far, especially Friday. It's not that unusual this time of my cycle but obviously I wondered if it was MM related.
In any case, I was losing my patience and decided to bust again Sunday afternoon, 4 days after my last. This time I took one fat mushroom, which I guess was at least double the original dose, chewed it for as long as I could before swallowing.
After 30 minutes it kicked in, at 45 minutes I was getting nervous that it was too much but it settled in after about an hour to a fairly intense buzz for about 4 hours. I certainly would not have wanted more buzz than I had.
Unfortunately, I had a headache about 2 hours in and needed to hit O2 but it quickly went away. Then, about 4 hours after that, I got hit hard with my nastiest CH this cycle, a Kip9. O2 didn't help, I was stumbling around moaning with eyes closed like a zombie, left eyes weeping liking mad, left sinus feeling like it was going to explode. Wow, that hurt.  With the Verapamil and O2 I haven't had to handle one of those for several cycles.
So now it's Tuesday, my Bday in fact. To celebrate, I went to get my blood taken and test my Vitamin D, Calcium, Magnesium and PTH levels and see where I am in the D3 regimen. I expect my 25 OH to be > 90 which is disappointing that it hasn't help that nasty part of my cycle.
My headaches last night were modest, got hit 3 times, all easily handled with O2. This is consistent for me, especially before my Verap/O2 days. If the beast hit me exceptionally hard I'd get a breather for a day, sometimes even two. Isn't that weird. Is it true for many of you? The Beast has a conscience? or physically whatever change the Beast makes via the pain is

This time around the headaches started coming back before I even finished my prednisone taper. Hi to everybody!
First thanks to all of you and the forum. I appreciate very much your efforts in fighting the`Beast`...
I am a EC since about 20 Years, having my first Cluster Attack after a root extraction of a tooth. My Episodes have been reliably precise of one Attack late at night for 2 months, followed by a remission period of also 2 months. For about the firs 14 years. as soon as the Prednisone is out of my body, the Pain starts again.

I first started getting cluster headaches 6 years ago, like you I thought the world needed to end, they lasted a total of 12 weeks, 6 weeks on the left then 6 weeks on the right. After numerous visits to the local GP  A&E and mountains of pain killers (which never worked) I was eventually referred to a specialist. It was then I was diagnosed with cluster headaches and prescribed verapamil. This prescription seemed to work after about a week but that was on week 12 of the attack..
3 years later the pain started again, I began to take the verapermill but with no relief. The specialist also mentioned pure oxygen would help, it didn't at all. Then a friend at work who knew I was suffering brought in an article on clusters and magic mushrooms. After reading it I was sold.
In a strange twist to the story, where I work, magic mushrooms grow and it was the season. I had in the past (younger days before children) enjoyed the wonder of the mushroom so couldn't quite believe this was going to be the answer to my living nightmare. I’m 38 and had my first ever cycle of cluster headaches this year, starting the first week of March. Preceding the cycle, I experienced three overlapping stressful events that spanned several months. First, I suffer from Raynaud’s syndrome, which has never been all that bad, except for the past winter, when it became MUCH worse than years past (moderately stressful due to impact on quality of life). It prompted blood work to confirm that the Raynaud’s was not secondary to a different, underlying problem. The results came back indicating that my Rheumatoid Factor was high, which validated that my Raynaud’s was secondary, confirming that I had some sort of autoimmune disease (heavy stress). This prompted another round of blood work to find out which autoimmune I was “blessed” with. The kicker was that it took a full week for the blood work to come back, which was more than enough time for me to research all of the potential autoimmune diseases it
could
be (HEAVY stress). It turned out to be Rheumatoid Arthritis and Sjogren’s syndrome which was probably one of the better outcomes.
Nearly immediately after the diagnosis, my wife and I signed a contract on a new house and had to put ours up for sale (more stress).
Throughout all of the above, at work, there was continuous HEAVY stress, as we worked on an account critical to our company.
Diagnosis:
Fortunately, my wife is a Nurse Practitioner and has a wealth of knowledge and access to subject matter experts, which ultimately led to my quick diagnosis & treatment. My treatment was a high-dose steroid taper (prednisone), starting at 60mg. The steroids stopped my attacks, but holy crap – my body does not handle high dose of steroids very well at all, but that’s a story for another time. I got in to see a headache specialist and was put on verapamil 320mg and got several abortive tools prescribed as well, O2, sumatriptan auto-injectors & zomig nasal spray.

I have not had a full attack since receiving the steroids. Do shadows bounce all over the head?
How do you know you’re out of cycle?
Is it possible that I’m still in cycle, but the verapamil is working?

# SIX-LOOP DIAGNOSTIC FRAMEWORK FOR CLUSTER HEADACHE
**A Personalized, Biomarker-Based Approach to Treatment**
Daniel Huddleston • 2026
---
## THE PROBLEM & THE SOLUTION: THE HOUSE FIRE ANALOGY
A cluster headache is up to six "fires" (loops) burning at once. Treating blind is like using the wrong extinguisher.
## THE PROTOCOL: TWO-DRAW BLOOD TEST
1. **BASELINE DRAW** (No Pain)
2. **CYCLE DRAW** (Active Pain)
3. **COMPARE & FIND THE "DELTA"**
4. **TREAT THE ACTIVE LOOPS**
Simple blood tests, ordered together, reveal which loops are running too hot.
---
## THE SIX LOOPS
### Loop 1: The Chain Reaction
**What it is & feels like:**
Nitric Oxide (NO) release triggers rapid spread. Feels like rapid-onset pain, escalates in seconds/minutes.
**Blood Test Markers:**
NO Metabolites (Nitrate/Nitrite)
**Targeted Treatments:**
High-flow oxygen, Triptans
---
### Loop 2: The Survival Shield
**What it is & feels like:**
Cell survival pathway activated, cells protect themselves. Feels like attacks return at same intensity daily.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Serum Tryptase
**Targeted Treatments:**
Prednisone (first round only)
---
### Loop 3: The Pain Relay
**What it is & feels like:**
CGRP protein carries pain signal, dilates vessels. Feels like intense pressure behind one eye, attacks chain together.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Serum CGRP
**Targeted Treatments:**
Emgality (galcanezumab)
---
### Loop 4: The Sensitivity Dial
**What it is & feels like:**
Histamine release sensitizes nerves and vessels. Feels like sinus congestion, fullness, allergy-like.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Plasma Histamine, Urine N-methylhistamine
**Targeted Treatments:**
Antihistamines, Mast cell stabilizers (e.g., ketotifen)
---
### Loop 5: The Stress Engine
**What it is & feels like:**
Hypothalamus releases stress hormone CRH. Feels like clockwork attacks, worse with stress, blood sugar spikes.
**Blood Test Markers:**
Cortisol, Fasting Glucose
**Targeted Treatments:**
Verapamil, Vitamin D3 regimen
---
### Loop 6: The Slow Burn
**What it is & feels like:**
Recruitment of inflammatory cells re-triggers mast cells. Feels like a bout that won't end, feeling "inflamed."
**Blood Test Markers:**
CRP, ESR, IL-6, TNF-alpha
**Targeted Treatments:**
Corticosteroids, Anti-inflammatory protocols
---
## WHY IT MATTERS
It is not "treatment resistance." The variation is measurable. The map connects tests to existing treatments.

Busting (treating CH with psychedelics, potentially at sub-hallucinogenic levels) is the reason this site exists, and it is a highly effective, low-side-effect way to end CH cycles and possibly prevent future cycles. Everyone with CH should consider it.
At the end of this post

I know I’m leaving out a lot. This should not be considered as a substitute for more thorough exploration. Just a starting point. I am hoping that others will correct, clarify, and amplify what’s here. One way to learn more about any of these topics is to put a word or words into the search bar at the top right of any page, and be sure that “all content” is checked. There’s a ton and a half of information here.
Doctors and Diagnoses
Most general-practice physicians are clueless about CH. Not only do they generally misdiagnose it, they don’t seem to have the energy or compassion to look up proper treatments when there is a correct diagnosis. Neurologists may be slightly better, but usually not much. Your best bet for diagnosis/treatment is a headache center, particularly one associated with a university.
Of course, a doctor’s treatment options are generally limited to pharmaceuticals. More about pharma treatments later.
You should have an MRI to rule out possible other causes of your pain.
There are a few conditions that are CH “lookalikes” that should be kept in mind, particularly if standard CH treatments (or busting) aren’t working. Probably the most common of these are hemicranias (which you can look up). Hemicranias are treatable with a drug called indomethacin, which should be tried if CH treatments aren’t working. There are also conditions caused by veins in the brain, called “venous headaches,” and cervicogenic headaches, which begin in the neck but create severe head pain. But if you have CH symptoms, the greatest likelihood is that you have CH.
Oxygen
You should have oxygen from a tank/cylinder/cannister,
not
a concentrator that makes O2 from room air. You should have either a
non-rebreather
mask or the mask that is made for people with CH, the “Cluster O2 Kit,” which you can buy here:
http://www.clusterheadaches.com/ccp8/
.
The flow rate should be sufficient that the reservoir bag on your mask is always full when you are ready to inhale using a forceful breathing strategy. For some people, this is 15 liters per minute (lpm); for others it can be higher. Since medical O2 suppliers generally won’t provide a regulator that goes higher than 15 lpm, you might have to get your own regulator. More about regulators below.
